content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

October 29, 2019


Jamie Raymond
Chief Deputy City Attorney
City of Corona
400 S. Vincentia Ave, Suite 310
Corona, CA 92882

Re:   Your Request for Advice
      Our File No. A-19-202

Dear Raymond:

     This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of
the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

     Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of
interest or Section 1090.

     Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1
FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for
additional advice.

QUESTION

     May a City Councilmember take part in decisions to approve entitlement requests that
would increase the potential number of residential lots that could be developed on a Subject Parcel
from 25 to 32, given that the Councilmember owns residential real property between 500 and 1,000
feet from the Subject Parcel?

CONCLUSION

     Yes, given the preliminary nature of the entitlement decisions and the intervening events
that would likely need to occur before any actual development occurred, it is not reasonably
foreseeable that the decisions would have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on the
Councilmember's property.

_______________________
¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-19-202
Page No. 2

FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER

The Corona-Norco Unified School District ("School District") is requesting entitlements
from the City of Corona ("City") to permit development of up to 32 single-family residential lots on
a 9.4-acre parcel of property within the City ("Subject Parcel"). The entitlements (collectively, the
"Entitlements") that the School District is requesting are as follows:

• General Plan Amendment: to change the land use designation of the Subject Parcel from
Estate Residential, which permits up to 3 dwelling units per acre, to Low Medium, which
permits 6-8 dwelling units per acre.
• South Corona Community Facilities Plan Amendment: to change the designation of
approximately one-third of the Subject Parcel from Estate, which has a target density of 1.47
dwelling units per acre, to Low Medium, which has a target density of 3.9 dwelling units per
acre. The remaining two-thirds of the Subject Parcel is already designated Low Medium.
• Zoning Amendment: to change: (1) approximately one-third of the Subject Parcel from R-1-
20, which requires a minimum lot area of 20,000 square feet, to R-1-7.2, which requires a
minimum lot area of 7,200 square feet; and (2) approximately two-thirds of the Subject
Parcel from R-1-14.4, which requires a minimum lot area of 14,400 square feet, to R-1-7.2,
which requires a minimum lot area of 7,200 square feet.

The existing General Plan and zoning designations for the Subject Parcel would permit up to
25 lots. The Entitlements would allow up to 32 residential lots to be established on the Subject
Parcel, 7 more than the current entitlements for the Subject Parcel. If the Entitlements are approved,
the School District has indicated an intent to subdivide the Subject Parcel into 32 residential lots
with an average lots size of 9,055 square feet.

City Councilmember Wes Speake owns real property, in which he has a direct investment
worth $2,000 or more, that is located approximately 717 feet from the Subject Parcel.
Councilmember Speake's property is located in the development that is immediately east of the
Subject Parcel and has a General Plan land use designation of Low Medium Density (6-8 du/acre), a
Community Facilities Plan designation of Low Medium (target density of 3.9 du/acre), and is zoned
R-1-7.2, with lot sizes of at least 7,200 square feet.

There are no streets connecting the Subject Parcel to the development where
Councilmember Speake's property is located. The Subject Parcel and the development where
Councilmember Speake's property is located are both geographically flat and there is no
meaningful grade differential.

In your request for advice, you also included five maps pertaining to the geographical area,
current land use designations, target densities, zoning designations, as well as the proposed
subdivision of the Subject Parcel into 32 lots.

You subsequently stated that while the existing General Plan and zoning designations for the
Subject Parcel would permit up to 25 lots on the Subject Parcel, the School District would need a
tract map to subdivide the Subject Parcel into 25 lots, which is subject to the approval of the City's
Planning Commission and City Council. Additionally, you noted that the School District had
indicated to City staff that it had previously issued a request for proposals for developers who might

File No. A-19-202
Page No. 3

want to purchase the Subject Parcel and develop it with 25 lots, but did not receive any responses,
and the School District is now pursuing the Entitlements to permit 32 lots. Approval of the
Entitlement requests at issue would not necessarily lead to the School District constructing a
residential development but would potentially lead to the School District again attempting to sell
the Subject Parcel.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 of the Act provides that "[n]o public official at any level of state or local
government shall make, participate in making or in any way attempt to use his official position to
influence a governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial
interest." "A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section
87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her
immediate family," or on certain economic interests, including "[a]ny real property in which the
public official has a direct or indirect interest worth two thousand dollars ($2,000) or more."
(Section 87103.)

When a public official's economic interest is explicitly involved in a governmental decision,
Regulation 18701(a) provides that "[a] financial effect on a financial interest is presumed to be
reasonably foreseeable if the financial interest is a named party in, or the subject of, a governmental
decision before the official or the official's agency. A financial interest is the subject of a
proceeding if the decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial, or revocation of any
license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract, with the financial interest, including any
decision affecting a property interest as described in Regulation 18702.2(a)(1)-(6)." (Regulation
18701(a).)

In this instance, Councilmember Speake's real property is not explicitly involved in the
decision. Thus, Regulation 18701(b) provides the standard for determining whether a decision's
financial effect on Councilmember Speake's real property is reasonably foreseeable. Regulation
18701(b) provides, "[a] financial effect need not be likely to be considered reasonably foreseeable.
In general, if the financial effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than
hypothetical or theoretical, it is reasonably foreseeable." However, "[i]f the financial result cannot
be expected absent extraordinary circumstances not subject to the public official's control, it is not
reasonably foreseeable."

Regulation 18701(b) also provides factors that should be considered in determining whether
a governmental decision will have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a financial interest
not explicitly involved in a decision. Among those factors, under Regulation 18701(b)(1), is "[t]he
extent to which the occurrence of the financial effect is contingent upon intervening events, not
including future governmental decisions by the official's agency, or any other agency appointed by
or subject to the budgetary control of the official's agency."

Here, the governmental decisions at issue would allow the Subject Parcel to potentially be
subdivided into 32 residential lots, rather than 25. However, based on the facts provided, no specific
development project is currently in place for the Subject Parcel. It is not evident that the decisions
at issue would necessarily result in the development of 32 new residences. Rather, it appears that

File No. A-19-202
Page No. 4

the School District was unable to sell the Subject Parcel with the prospect of it being subdivided
into 25 lots, and the District now believes, or hopes, that it may be able to sell the Subject Parcel if
it could potentially be divided into 32 lots. No facts have been presented that indicate whether that
belief is well-founded, or if-and-when the Subject Parcel will ultimately be developed. At the
moment, it is only certain that if the City Council were to approve the Entitlement requests, then at
some point in the future, 32 residences could be developed on the Subject Parcel, rather than 25.
Based on such limited information, and the appearance that actual development of the Subject
Parcel is contingent on significant intervening events—that is, the School District's ability to find a
purchaser who would then establish a development plan—it is not reasonably foreseeable that
decisions immediately before the City Council would have a financial effect on Councilmember
Speake's property.

We emphasize that this advice is based on the limited facts provided, which indicate the
Entitlement decisions before the City Council are preliminary and do not involve an existing project
or development, and our advice is specifically related to the Entitlement decisions currently before
the City Council. In the future, if an additional decision related to the Subject Parcel comes before
the City Council, that separate decision may still present a disqualifying conflict of interest under
the Act, depending on the nature of the decision and the available facts.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

[signature]

By: Kevin Cornwall
Counsel, Legal Division

KMC:aja

May a City Councilmember take part in decisions to approve entitlement requests that

feet from the Subject Parcel?

foreseeable that the decisions would have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on the 
Councilmember's property.

QUESTION: May a City Councilmember take part in decisions to approve entitlement requests that 
would increase the potential number of residential lots that could be developed on a Subject Parcel 
from 25 to 32, given that the Councilmember owns residential real property between 500 and 1,000 
feet from the Subject Parcel?

CONCLUSION: Yes, given the preliminary nature of the entitlement decisions and the intervening events 
that would likely need to occur before any actual development occurred, it is not reasonably 
foreseeable that the decisions would have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on the 
Councilmember's property.
